meta.title: Rebid After 1NT Response
meta.description: Partner has 6-10, no fit -- what now?

blocks.content:
## Partner Has Limited Their Hand

When partner responds 1NT to your opening bid, they've told you a clear story: **6-10 points, no support for your suit, and no new suit they could bid at the 1-level.** This is a limited, non-forcing bid ‚Äî you could pass it.

Because partner has narrowed their hand so precisely, **you are now the captain.** You know partner's approximate range, so you can calculate the combined strength and make the final decision.

## Your Options

### Pass (Balanced Minimum)
With 12-15 points and a balanced hand, the combined total is at most 25. Game is unlikely. Pass and play 1NT.

**Example:** You opened 1‚ô• with ‚ôÝKJ5 ‚ô•AQ874 ‚ô¶Q93 ‚ô£J4 (14 HCP).
Partner responds 1NT. Your hand is semi-balanced with only 5 hearts. **Pass**. 1NT may be the best spot.

### Rebid Your Suit (6+ Cards, Minimum)
With 6+ cards in your suit and a minimum hand, rebid your suit at the 2-level. Partner's 1NT denied a fit, but with 6 cards you know you have at least a 6-card suit for trump. Playing in 2M will usually produce more tricks than 1NT.

**Example:** You opened 1‚ôÝ with ‚ôÝAQJ974 ‚ô•K3 ‚ô¶J85 ‚ô£64 (12 HCP).
Partner responds 1NT. Rebid **2‚ôÝ**. With a 6-card spade suit, 2‚ôÝ will play much better than 1NT.

### Bid a New Suit (Unbalanced, Showing 4+ Cards)
With an unbalanced hand and a second suit, bid it. This shows 4+ cards in the new suit and does **not** promise extra values ‚Äî it's just looking for a better fit.

**Example:** You opened 1‚ôÝ with ‚ôÝKQJ84 ‚ô•A3 ‚ô¶Q9754 ‚ô£8 (13 HCP).
Partner responds 1NT. Bid **2‚ô¶** ‚Äî showing your second suit. Partner can choose between spades, diamonds, or pass.

Important: bidding a **lower-ranking** new suit is not forcing. Partner can pass. But bidding a **higher-ranking** new suit (a reverse at the 2-level) would show 17+ points.

### Bid 2NT (18-19 Points, Balanced)
With 18-19 HCP and a balanced hand, invite game by bidding 2NT. Partner will pass with a minimum (6-7) or bid 3NT with a maximum (8-10).

**Example:** You opened 1‚ô• with ‚ôÝAK5 ‚ô•AQ874 ‚ô¶KJ3 ‚ô£Q4 (19 HCP).
Partner responds 1NT. Bid **2NT** ‚Äî invitational. Combined points: 24-29. Partner decides.

### Jump Rebid or New Suit (Extra Values)
With 16-18 points and a 6+ card suit, jump rebid to the 3-level.

**Example:** You opened 1‚ô• with ‚ôÝK3 ‚ô•AKQ974 ‚ô¶AJ5 ‚ô£84 (17 HCP).
Partner responds 1NT. Jump to **3‚ô•** ‚Äî invitational with a strong 6-card suit.

### Bid 3NT (20-21 Points, Balanced)
With 20-21 HCP balanced, the combined total is at least 26. Bid game.

## Decision Summary

| Your Hand | Points | Rebid |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Balanced minimum | 12-15 | Pass |
| 6+ card suit, minimum | 12-15 | Rebid suit at 2-level |
| Two-suited, minimum | 12-15 | Bid new (lower) suit |
| 6+ card suit, medium | 16-18 | Jump rebid (3-level) |
| Balanced, medium | 18-19 | 2NT (invitational) |
| Balanced, maximum | 20-21 | 3NT |

## Key Insight: You're in Charge

After a 1NT response, opener knows both hands' approximate strength. Responder has given up control by bidding 1NT ‚Äî they've said "I have 6-10 and nothing exciting." Now it's up to you to steer the partnership to the right contract.

problems.question: You opened 1‚ôÝ with ‚ôÝAQT84 ‚ô•K73 ‚ô¶Q95 ‚ô£J4 (13 HCP, 5 spades). Partner responds 1NT. What do you rebid?
problems.explanation: 13 HCP, only 5 spades, semi-balanced. Pass -- you don't have 6 spades to rebid and you don't have the strength for anything else. 1NT may be fine.

problems.question: You opened 1‚ô• with ‚ôÝ8 ‚ô•AKJ974 ‚ô¶K83 ‚ô£Q52 (14 HCP, 6 hearts). Partner responds 1NT. What do you rebid?
problems.explanation: 6-card heart suit with minimum points. Rebid 2‚ô• -- partner didn't support hearts, but with 6 cards your suit will play well as trump.

problems.question: You opened 1‚ôÝ with ‚ôÝAK873 ‚ô•AQ ‚ô¶KJ4 ‚ô£Q93 (19 HCP, balanced). Partner responds 1NT. What do you rebid?
problems.explanation: 19 HCP, balanced. Bid 2NT -- invitational. Partner will bid 3NT with 8-10 or pass with 6-7. (Note: this is borderline; some would bid 3NT directly.)

problems.question: You opened 1‚ô• with ‚ôÝ4 ‚ô•AQ853 ‚ô¶KJ974 ‚ô£K8 (14 HCP, 5-5). Partner responds 1NT. What do you rebid?
problems.explanation: Unbalanced with 5 hearts and 5 diamonds. Bid 2‚ô¶ to show your second suit. Partner can choose between hearts, diamonds, or notrump.

reviewQuestions.question: After you open 1‚ôÝ and partner responds 1NT, who decides where the partnership plays?
reviewQuestions.explanation: After 1NT, responder has fully described their hand (6-10, no fit). Opener now knows both hands and becomes the captain.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! When partner limits their hand, you take the wheel. You know the most about both hands.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: After 1NT, responder has limited their hand (6-10), so opener knows enough about both hands to be the captain and make the final decision.

reviewQuestions.question: You opened 1‚ô• with a minimum hand and a 6-card heart suit. Partner responds 1NT. What should you do?
options: Rebid 2‚ô• -- your 6-card suit will play better
reviewQuestions.explanation: With a 6-card suit, rebid 2‚ô•. Even though partner didn't support hearts, your 6 cards mean hearts will likely play better than 1NT.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's right! Six cards in your suit is too good to leave in 1NT -- rebid it at the 2-level.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: With 6 hearts, rebid 2‚ô•. Partner didn't raise, but with 6 cards your suit is long enough to be the best trump fit.
